name: पुरुषोत्तमयोग
teaching: The world is an aśvattha tree grown upside down — roots above in the unseen, branches below nourished by the guṇas, and no end of it visible from inside. Krishna's instruction is severe and brief: cut it with the axe of non-attachment, then seek the place from which no one returns. A fragment of the divine lives as every soul; the whole exceeds both the perishable and the imperishable. An inverted tree, an axe of non-attachment, a fragment of God wearing senses, a fire digesting every meal — and above the perishable and imperishable alike, the Person this whole song belongs to.
questions: What are the deepest roots of your world-tree — the assumptions everything else branches from?
Non-attachment is called an axe, not a mood. What would one deliberate cut look like?
If a fragment of the supreme is living as you, how does that change what self-respect means?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
ऊर्ध्वमूलमधःशाखमश्वत्थं प्राहुरव्ययम् ।
छन्दांसि यस्य पर्णानि यस्तं वेद स वेदवित्॥
verses.transliteration:
ūrdhvamūlamadhaḥśākhamaśvatthaṃ prāhuravyayam
chandāṃsi yasya parṇāni yastaṃ veda sa vedavit
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: they speak of the imperishable ashvattha tree, its roots above, its branches below, whose leaves are the Vedic hymns. One who knows it is a knower of the Veda.
verses.translation: They (the wise) speak of the indestructible peepul tree having its root above and branches below, whose leaves are the metres or hymns: he who knows it is a knower of the Vedas.

anvaya.meaning: with roots above, branches below
the ashvattha, they call imperishable
whose leaves are the Vedic hymns
who knows it — that one is a Veda-knower

verses.explanation: The chapter opens with the strangest tree in scripture: an ashvattha — the sacred fig — growing upside down, rooted in the Above, branching downward into the world. The cosmos as inverted tree: nourishment flows from a root we cannot see, and what looks like the ground of things is actually the foliage. The leaves are the Vedas themselves — even revelation is part of the canopy, not the root. Urdhva-mulam adhah-shakham — the inverted ashvattha, roots above, branches below: the image the Katha Upanishad (6.1) holds and the Gita unfolds. The commentaries read the botany closely: nourishment descends from an unseen root; the visible world is canopy, not ground. Chandamsi yasya parnani — the Vedas as leaves: even scripture photosynthesizes for the tree; it is not the root. Yas tam veda sa veda-vit: to know the tree's true orientation is to know what the Vedas are for.
verses.connection: Opens the chapter's master-image (15.1–4): existence as a tree to be understood, then felled.
verses.reflection: Picture the inverted tree once today: nourishment descending from a root above — and locate your sap.
verses.background: Krishna opens with the tradition's strangest tree: the eternal ashvattha with roots above and branches below, whose leaves are the Vedic hymns — its form not perceivable here, no end, no beginning, no resting place. Cut this firm-rooted tree with the strong axe of non-attachment, he commands, and seek that goal from which none return, surrendering to the primal Person.

verses.devanagari:
अधश्चोर्ध्वं प्रसृतास्तस्य शाखा
गुणप्रवृद्धा विषयप्रवालाः ।
अधश्च मूलान्यनुसन्ततानि
कर्मानुबन्धीनि मनुष्यलोके॥
verses.transliteration:
adhaścordhvaṃ prasṛtāstasya śākhā guṇapravṛddhā viṣayapravālāḥ
adhaśca mūlānyanusantatāni karmānubandhīni manuṣyaloke
verses.essence: Below and above its branches spread, nourished by the gunas, with sense-objects for shoots; and downward its roots trail into the world of men, binding by action.
verses.translation: Below and above spread its branches, nourished by the Gunas; sense-objects are its buds; and below, in the world of men, stretch forth the roots, originating action.

anvaya.meaning: below and above, spread out
its branches, nourished by the gunas
with sense-objects as buds
binding-by-karma, in the world of men

verses.explanation: The botany of bondage: branches fattened by the three gunas of chapter 14, tipped with viṣaya-pravālāḥ — sense-objects as tender new shoots, each enticement a fresh bud. And a second root-system: aerial roots, like the banyan's, dropping back down into human life and re-anchoring there as karma. The tree propagates through our actions; every grasping deed is a root striking soil.
verses.connection: Maps ch14's gunas onto the tree: how the world-growth feeds, buds, and re-roots itself in us.
verses.reflection: Spot one fresh bud of enticement today — a viṣaya-shoot — and watch it without watering it.
verses.background: Krishna opens with the tradition's strangest tree: the eternal ashvattha with roots above and branches below, whose leaves are the Vedic hymns — its form not perceivable here, no end, no beginning, no resting place. Cut this firm-rooted tree with the strong axe of non-attachment, he commands, and seek that goal from which none return, surrendering to the primal Person.

verses.devanagari:
न रूपमस्येह तथोपलभ्यते
नान्तो न चादिर्न च सम्प्रतिष्ठा ।
अश्वत्थमेनं सुविरूढमूलं
असङ्गशस्त्रेण दृढेन छित्त्वा॥
verses.transliteration:
na rūpamasyeha tathopalabhyate nānto na cādirna ca sampratiṣṭhā
aśvatthamenaṃ suvirūḍhamūlaṃ asaṅgaśastreṇa dṛḍhena chittvā
verses.essence: Its form is not perceived here as such — not its end, nor its beginning, nor its foundation. Having cut this firm-rooted ashvattha with the strong axe of non-attachment —
verses.translation: Its form is not perceived here as such, neither its end nor its origin, nor its foundation nor resting place: having cut asunder this firmly rooted peepul tree with the strong axe of non-attachment.

anvaya.word: न रूपम् अस्य इह तथा उपलभ्यते
anvaya.meaning: its form is not thus perceived here

anvaya.word: न अन्तः न च आदिः न च सम्प्रतिष्ठा
anvaya.meaning: no end, no beginning, no foundation
this ashvattha, with well-grown roots
having cut with the firm axe of non-attachment

verses.explanation: Standing inside the canopy, no one can see the whole tree — no edge, no origin, no trunk to point to; we are leaves trying to survey the forest. So the instruction is not to map it but to fell it: asaṅga-śastreṇa, with the axe of non-attachment, dṛḍhena, swung firm. The one tree in scripture that is sacred *and* must be cut down — because what it grows is entanglement.
verses.connection: The image turns from contemplation to action: the unsurveyable tree meets the axe of 15.3–4.
verses.reflection: Stop trying to map the whole forest today; sharpen the axe of non-attachment on one branch instead.
verses.background: Krishna opens with the tradition's strangest tree: the eternal ashvattha with roots above and branches below, whose leaves are the Vedic hymns — its form not perceivable here, no end, no beginning, no resting place. Cut this firm-rooted tree with the strong axe of non-attachment, he commands, and seek that goal from which none return, surrendering to the primal Person.

verses.devanagari:
ततः पदं तत्परिमार्गितव्यं
यस्मिन्गता न निवर्तन्ति भूयः ।
तमेव चाद्यं पुरुषं प्रपद्ये ।
यतः प्रवृत्तिः प्रसृता पुराणी॥
verses.transliteration:
tataḥ padaṃ tatparimārgitavyaṃ yasmingatā na nivartanti bhūyaḥ
tameva cādyaṃ puruṣaṃ prapadye
yataḥ pravṛttiḥ prasṛtā purāṇī
verses.essence: — then that goal must be sought, going to which none return again: 'I take refuge in that primal Purusha from whom the ancient current streamed forth.'
verses.translation: Then That goal should be sought for, whither having gone none returns again. I seek refuge in that Primeval Purusha Whence streamed forth the ancient activity or energy.

anvaya.meaning: then that goal is to be sought out
gone to which, they return no more

anvaya.word: तम् एव च आद्यं पुरुषं प्रपद्ये
anvaya.meaning: 'in that very primal Purusha I take refuge'
from whom the ancient activity streamed forth

verses.explanation: After the felling, the seeking — and the verse hands the seeker the exact words: tam eva cādyaṁ puruṣaṁ prapadye, *I take refuge in the primal Person*. A mantra of surrender embedded mid-teaching, present tense, first person, ready to say. The destination is defined by its one-way door: yasmin gatā na nivartanti, no return — the same promise as 8.21, now reached by axe and refuge together.
verses.connection: Completes the tree-teaching: cut the growth, then trace the sap back to the root and surrender there.
verses.reflection: Say the planted mantra once, slowly: I take refuge in that primal Purusha — and mean the present tense.
verses.background: Krishna opens with the tradition's strangest tree: the eternal ashvattha with roots above and branches below, whose leaves are the Vedic hymns — its form not perceivable here, no end, no beginning, no resting place. Cut this firm-rooted tree with the strong axe of non-attachment, he commands, and seek that goal from which none return, surrendering to the primal Person.

verses.devanagari:
निर्मानमोहा जितसङ्गदोषा
अध्यात्मनित्या विनिवृत्तकामाः ।
द्वन्द्वैर्विमुक्ताः सुखदुःखसंज्ञैर्-
गच्छन्त्यमूढाः पदमव्ययं तत्॥
verses.transliteration:
nirmānamohā jitasaṅgadoṣā adhyātmanityā vinivṛttakāmāḥ
dvandvairvimuktāḥ sukhaduḥkhasaṃjñaira- gacchantyamūḍhāḥ padamavyayaṃ tat
verses.essence: Free of pride and delusion, the fault of attachment conquered, ever dwelling in the Self, desires fully turned away, released from the pairs called pleasure and pain — the undeluded reach that imperishable goal.
verses.translation: Free from pride and delusion, victorious over the evil of attachment, dwelling constantly in the Self, their desires having completely turned away, freed from the pairs of opposites known as pleasure and pain, the undeluded reach the eternal goal.

anvaya.meaning: without pride-delusion, attachment's fault conquered
constant in the Self, desires turned away
freed from the pairs named pleasure-pain
the undeluded go to that imperishable goal

verses.explanation: The traveler's profile for the road of 15.4 — five released burdens in a single breath: pride, delusion, clinging, craving, and the seesaw of the pairs. Each item is something *put down*, not acquired; the journey to the imperishable is packed entirely by unpacking. Amūḍhāḥ, the undeluded: the only positive credential is clear sight.
verses.connection: Lists the equipment for 15.4's seeking — all of it subtraction.
verses.reflection: Pack for the journey by unpacking today: set down pride, delusion, clinging — travel light.
verses.background: The travelers and the destination are described in two breaths: free of pride and delusion, attachment conquered, desires stilled, released from the pairs of pleasure and pain, the undeluded reach the imperishable goal — which neither sun nor moon nor fire illumines; having gone there, none return; that is My supreme abode.

verses.devanagari:
न तद्भासयते सूर्यो न शशाङ्को न पावकः ।
यद्गत्वा न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम॥
verses.transliteration:
na tadbhāsayate sūryo na śaśāṅko na pāvakaḥ
yadgatvā na nivartante taddhāma paramaṃ mama
verses.essence: Neither sun nor moon nor fire illumines it. Having gone there, none return: that is my supreme abode.
verses.translation: Neither doth the sun illumine there nor the moon, nor the fire; having gone thither they return not; that is My supreme abode.

anvaya.meaning: that, the sun does not illumine
nor the moon, nor fire
going whither, they return not
that is my supreme abode

verses.explanation: The destination described by what it does not need: sun, moon, fire — the three lamps of the manifest world — all unemployed there, because it is the light they borrow from. The Upanishads' great verse (Katha, Mundaka) quoted into the Gita: na tatra sūryo bhāti. Where light itself is at home, nothing needs lighting; and from home, no one commutes back.
verses.connection: Names the journey's end — mama dhāma, *my* abode — and quietly personalizes the Upanishadic absolute.
verses.reflection: Spend a minute on the light that lamps cannot reach — sun, moon, fire all unemployed there.
verses.background: The travelers and the destination are described in two breaths: free of pride and delusion, attachment conquered, desires stilled, released from the pairs of pleasure and pain, the undeluded reach the imperishable goal — which neither sun nor moon nor fire illumines; having gone there, none return; that is My supreme abode.

verses.devanagari:
ममैवांशो जीवलोके जीवभूतः सनातनः ।
मनःषष्ठानीन्द्रियाणि प्रकृतिस्थानि कर्षति॥
verses.transliteration:
mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke jīvabhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ
manaḥṣaṣṭhānīndriyāṇi prakṛtisthāni karṣati
verses.essence: A fragment of my very self, become the eternal living soul in the world of life, draws to itself the senses, with mind as the sixth, which rest in prakriti.
verses.translation: An eternal portion of Myself having become a living soul in the world of life, draws to (itself) the (five) senses with the mind for the sixth, abiding in Nature.

anvaya.meaning: a fragment of my very self, in the world of life
become the jiva, eternal
the senses, with mind as sixth
resting in prakriti, it draws

verses.explanation: The verse that names what you are: mamaivāṁśaḥ — a fragment, a portion, of *me*; and sanātanaḥ — eternally so, not a temporary splinter awaiting reabsorption into anonymity. Then the embodiment mechanism in one verb: karṣati, the soul *draws* — pulls mind and senses around itself like a magnet gathering filings, and a person assembles. Every individual life is divinity, fractionally present, dressing itself in instruments. Mamaivamsho jiva-loke — a fragment of My very self, eternal, become the living soul: the verse every school reads as its own birth certificate of the jiva. Sanatanah is the word the debates turn on — the fragment is eternal as fragment (Madhva, Ramanuja: real, everlasting individuality) or eternal in essence while the fragmentation is appearance (Shankara: space in jars). Manah-shashthani indriyani karshati: whichever metaphysics, the daily fact stands — the divine portion in you is what gathers a mind and five senses each morning and calls the bundle a person.
verses.connection: The chapter's anthropology: after the cosmic tree, the soul itself — divine in substance, derivative in scale.
verses.schools_note: The amsha doctrine divides the schools at their root. Advaita reads 'fragment' as figurative — the one Self appearing partitioned, as space in jars. Ramanuja takes it literally and structurally: the soul is a real, eternal mode of God, distinct yet inseparable. Madhva sharpens further: an eternal reflection, never identical. All agree on the wonder: the jiva's pedigree is divine.
verses.reflection: Carry your pedigree through one hard hour: an eternal fragment of His very self — that is the traveler.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the traveler itself: an eternal fragment of My very self, become the living soul, draws to itself mind and the five senses; taking a body and leaving it, the lord of the body carries these along as wind carries fragrance from their seats. The deluded do not perceive him departing or dwelling; those with the eye of knowledge see — the striving yogis behold him in themselves; the unrefined, though striving, see him not.

verses.devanagari:
शरीरं यदवाप्नोति यच्चाप्युत्क्रामतीश्वरः ।
गृहीत्वैतानि संयाति वायुर्गन्धानिवाशयात्॥
verses.transliteration:
śarīraṃ yadavāpnoti yaccāpyutkrāmatīśvaraḥ
gṛhitvaitāni saṃyāti vāyurgandhānivāśayāt
verses.essence: When the lord of the body takes on a body, and when he leaves it, he departs taking these along — as the wind carries scents from their dwelling-places.
verses.translation: When the Lord (as the individual soul) obtains a body and when He leaves it, He takes these and goes (with them) as the wind takes the scents from their seats (flowers, etc.).

anvaya.meaning: whatever body he obtains
and from whichever the lord departs
taking these, he goes forth
as the wind, scents from their seat

verses.explanation: Transmigration in a single fragrance: vāyur gandhān ivāśayāt — as wind lifts perfume from a flower bed and carries it elsewhere, the soul lifts mind and senses from the dying body and moves on. The scent is invisible, weightless, yet unmistakably *that garden's*. What travels between lives is just so: no luggage, only the subtle signature of all one has been.
verses.connection: Shows 15.7's gathered instruments in transit — the mechanics of rebirth, rendered as perfume.
verses.reflection: Notice what subtle scent you would carry from this chapter of life — the wind takes only fragrance.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the traveler itself: an eternal fragment of My very self, become the living soul, draws to itself mind and the five senses; taking a body and leaving it, the lord of the body carries these along as wind carries fragrance from their seats. The deluded do not perceive him departing or dwelling; those with the eye of knowledge see — the striving yogis behold him in themselves; the unrefined, though striving, see him not.

verses.devanagari:
श्रोत्रं चक्षुः स्पर्शनं च रसनं घ्राणमेव च ।
अधिष्ठाय मनश्चायं विषयानुपसेवते॥
verses.transliteration:
śrotraṃ cakṣuḥ sparśanaṃ ca rasanaṃ ghrāṇameva ca
adhiṣṭhāya manaścāyaṃ viṣayānupasevate
verses.essence: Presiding over hearing, sight, touch, taste and smell, and over the mind, this one savors the objects of the senses.
verses.translation: Presiding over the ear, the eye, touch, taste and smell, as well as the mind, it enjoys the objects of the senses.

anvaya.meaning: hearing, sight, and touch
taste, and smell
presiding over (these) and the mind, this one
enjoys the sense-objects

verses.explanation: The fragment settles into its new instrument and the concert begins: adhiṣṭhāya, presiding — the soul as the one audience for whom five senses and a mind perform the world. Every flavor, every birdsong, every touch is staged for this hidden enjoyer. The verse holds no rebuke; it simply identifies who is actually tasting your tea.
verses.connection: The soul installed and operating — which makes 15.10's blindness to it all the stranger.
verses.reflection: Identify the real taster of your tea once today: the hidden enjoyer presiding over the senses.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the traveler itself: an eternal fragment of My very self, become the living soul, draws to itself mind and the five senses; taking a body and leaving it, the lord of the body carries these along as wind carries fragrance from their seats. The deluded do not perceive him departing or dwelling; those with the eye of knowledge see — the striving yogis behold him in themselves; the unrefined, though striving, see him not.

verses.devanagari:
उत्क्रामन्तं स्थितं वापि भुञ्जानं वा गुणान्वितम् ।
विमूढा नानुपश्यन्ति पश्यन्ति ज्ञानचक्षुषः॥
verses.transliteration:
utkrāmantaṃ sthitaṃ vāpi bhuñjānaṃ vā guṇānvitam
vimūḍhā nānupaśyanti paśyanti jñānacakṣuṣaḥ
verses.essence: Whether departing, staying, or experiencing, joined with the gunas — the deluded do not perceive him. Those with the eye of knowledge see.
verses.translation: The deluded do not see Him Who departs, stays and enjoys; but they who possess the eye of knowledge behold Him.

anvaya.meaning: departing, or even staying
or experiencing, joined with the gunas
the deluded do not perceive (him)
those with the eye of knowledge see

verses.explanation: The strangest blindness: the soul is the nearest fact there is — it is doing the looking — and precisely so, the deluded never notice it, in life, in experience, even at the bedside of death. Like the eye that sees everything but itself. The remedy is named from 13.35: jñāna-cakṣus, the knowledge-eye, which turns perception around to catch the perceiver.
verses.connection: The visibility problem: the divine fragment of 15.7 hides in plain sight as the seer itself.
verses.reflection: Turn perception around once today and try to catch the perceiver — the nearest hide in plain sight.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the traveler itself: an eternal fragment of My very self, become the living soul, draws to itself mind and the five senses; taking a body and leaving it, the lord of the body carries these along as wind carries fragrance from their seats. The deluded do not perceive him departing or dwelling; those with the eye of knowledge see — the striving yogis behold him in themselves; the unrefined, though striving, see him not.

verses.devanagari:
यतन्तो योगिनश्चैनं पश्यन्त्यात्मन्यवस्थितम् ।
यतन्तोऽप्यकृतात्मानो नैनं पश्यन्त्यचेतसः॥
verses.transliteration:
yatanto yoginaścainaṃ paśyantyātmanyavasthitam
yatanto'pyakṛtātmāno nainaṃ paśyantyacetasaḥ
verses.essence: Striving yogis behold him established in the self; but the unrefined, the unmindful, though striving, see him not.
verses.translation: The Yogins striving (for perfection) behold Him dwelling in the Self; but, the unrefined and unintelligent, even though striving, see Him not.

anvaya.meaning: and striving yogis — him
behold, established in the self
though striving, those of unmade self
do not see him — the unmindful

verses.explanation: A precision about effort: yatanto 'pi — *even striving*, some see nothing. The variable is akṛtātmānaḥ, the un-made self: effort poured into an unprepared instrument is rain on stone. The twenty marks of 13.8–12 were the making; vision is what a made self does naturally. Practice without purification gathers technique and misses the point.
verses.connection: Qualifies 15.10's knowledge-eye: it must be grown in a refined self, not merely exercised.
verses.reflection: Pair your striving with refining today — effort lands only in a made self; polish the instrument.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the traveler itself: an eternal fragment of My very self, become the living soul, draws to itself mind and the five senses; taking a body and leaving it, the lord of the body carries these along as wind carries fragrance from their seats. The deluded do not perceive him departing or dwelling; those with the eye of knowledge see — the striving yogis behold him in themselves; the unrefined, though striving, see him not.

verses.devanagari:
यदादित्यगतं तेजो जगद्भासयतेऽखिलम् ।
यच्चन्द्रमसि यच्चाग्नौ तत्तेजो विद्धि मामकम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yadādityagataṃ tejo jagadbhāsayate'khilam
yaccandramasi yaccāgnau tattejo viddhi māmakam
verses.essence: The splendor residing in the sun, which illumines the entire world, and that in the moon and in fire — know that splendor as mine.
verses.translation: That light which residing in the sun illumines the whole world, that which is in the moon and in the fire know that light to be Mine.

anvaya.meaning: the splendor residing in the sun
which illumines the world entire
that in the moon, and that in fire
that splendor — know as mine

verses.explanation: The chapter turns outward: the three great lights that 15.6 said cannot reach his abode are now claimed as his projections — every lumen in the visible universe drawn from one account. The teaching method is immersive: for the next four verses, wherever Arjuna's senses land — light, earth, food, memory — they will be told whose presence they are touching.
verses.connection: Begins the immanence-litany (15.12–15): the Purushottama located in daylight, soil, digestion, and thought.
verses.reflection: Read every lumen today off one account: sun, moon, fire — splendor on loan from a single source.
verses.background: The immanence litany of the chapter: the splendor in sun, moon, and fire is Mine; entering the earth I support beings; becoming the moon I nourish the plants; as the fire of life in every body I digest the fourfold food; and seated in every heart I give memory, knowledge, and their taking away — by all the Vedas I alone am to be known, the maker and knower of Vedanta.

verses.devanagari:
गामाविश्य च भूतानि धारयाम्यहमोजसा ।
पुष्णामि चौषधीः सर्वाः सोमो भूत्वा रसात्मकः॥
verses.transliteration:
gāmāviśya ca bhūtāni dhārayāmyahamojasā
puṣṇāmi cauṣadhīḥ sarvāḥ somo bhūtvā rasātmakaḥ
verses.essence: Entering the earth, I sustain all beings by my power; and becoming the sap-rich soma, I nourish all the herbs.
verses.translation: Permeating the earth I support all beings by (My) energy; and having become the watery moon I nourish all herbs.

anvaya.meaning: and entering the earth, the beings
I sustain by my vigor
and I nourish all the herbs
becoming soma, whose essence is sap

verses.explanation: From light to ground: the planet's very holding-together is his ojas, and the moon — soma, lord of sap in Vedic physiology — is his nourishing mode, swelling the herbs with juice by night. Agriculture becomes theology: the wheat ripens because someone is in the field, in the moonlight, in the rising water of the stalk.
verses.connection: The litany descends from sky to soil — and prepares the most intimate claim of all in 15.14.
verses.reflection: Walk on the earth once today aware of what holds it together — and what swells the green things by night.
verses.background: The immanence litany of the chapter: the splendor in sun, moon, and fire is Mine; entering the earth I support beings; becoming the moon I nourish the plants; as the fire of life in every body I digest the fourfold food; and seated in every heart I give memory, knowledge, and their taking away — by all the Vedas I alone am to be known, the maker and knower of Vedanta.

verses.devanagari:
अहं वैश्वानरो भूत्वा प्राणिनां देहमाश्रितः ।
प्राणापानसमायुक्तः पचाम्यन्नं चतुर्विधम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ahaṃ vaiśvānaro bhūtvā prāṇināṃ dehamāśritaḥ
prāṇāpānasamāyuktaḥ pacāmyannaṃ caturvidham
verses.essence: Becoming the fire of life in the bodies of breathing beings, joined with the in-breath and out-breath, I digest the four kinds of food.
verses.translation: Having become the fire Vaisvanara, I abide in the body of living beings and, associated with the Prana and the Apana, digest the fourfold food.

anvaya.meaning: I, becoming the vaishvanara (fire)
dwelling in the body of breathing beings
joined with prana and apana
I digest the fourfold food

verses.explanation: The litany arrives inside the belly: vaiśvānara, the universal fire, here the gastric flame itself, working the bellows of prāṇa and apāna to cook the four foods — chewed, drunk, licked, sucked. The God of the thousand suns of chapter 11, found again as the warmth in your stomach after lunch. Many recite this verse before meals: digestion acknowledged as the nearest daily miracle.
verses.connection: Immanence at its most domestic: the cosmic fire as the body's own hearth.
verses.reflection: Before one meal today, greet the vaishvanara fire — the cosmic flame doing your digestion.
verses.background: The immanence litany of the chapter: the splendor in sun, moon, and fire is Mine; entering the earth I support beings; becoming the moon I nourish the plants; as the fire of life in every body I digest the fourfold food; and seated in every heart I give memory, knowledge, and their taking away — by all the Vedas I alone am to be known, the maker and knower of Vedanta.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वस्य चाहं हृदि सन्निविष्टो
मत्तः स्मृतिर्ज्ञानमपोहनञ्च ।
वेदैश्च सर्वैरहमेव वेद्यो
वेदान्तकृद्वेदविदेव चाहम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvasya cāhaṃ hṛdi sanniviṣṭo mattaḥ smṛtirjñānamapohanañca
vedaiśca sarvairahameva vedyo vedāntakṛdvedavideva cāham
verses.essence: And I am seated in the hearts of all; from me come memory, knowledge, and their loss. By all the Vedas it is I who am to be known; I am the maker of Vedanta, and the knower of the Veda am I.
verses.translation: And I am seated in the hearts of all; from Me are memory and knowledge, as well as their absence. I am verily That which has to be known by all the Vedas; I am indeed the author of the Vedanta and the knower of the Vedas am I.

anvaya.meaning: and I am seated in the heart of all
from me: memory, knowledge, and (their) removal

anvaya.word: वेदैः च सर्वैः अहम् एव वेद्यः
anvaya.meaning: by all the Vedas, I alone am to be known
the maker of Vedanta, and the Veda-knower, am I

verses.explanation: The litany's summit, and one of the Gita's most quoted verses. Seated in every heart, he is the source of smṛti and jñāna — and, startlingly, of apohanam, *forgetting* too: even the veil is his issue, which is why only he can lift it. Then the hermeneutic key: all scripture, read to its end, is about him — he wrote the conclusion (vedānta-kṛt) and knows what every verse was for. Sarvasya chaham hridi sannivishtah — seated in the hearts of all: the verse recited before study across the tradition, since it claims the whole curriculum. Three gifts from one resident — smritir jnanam apohanam cha — memory, knowledge, and forgetting: the commentaries pause at the third; even the veiling is His office, which is why only He can lift it. Vedaish cha sarvair aham eva vedyah: the hermeneutic master key — all scripture, read to the end, is about the one reading it from within.
verses.connection: Closes the litany at its center — the heart — and sets up the final doctrine of the two-plus-one purushas.
verses.reflection: Sit with the heart-resident a moment: source of your memory, your knowledge — and yes, your forgetting.
verses.background: The immanence litany of the chapter: the splendor in sun, moon, and fire is Mine; entering the earth I support beings; becoming the moon I nourish the plants; as the fire of life in every body I digest the fourfold food; and seated in every heart I give memory, knowledge, and their taking away — by all the Vedas I alone am to be known, the maker and knower of Vedanta.

verses.devanagari:
द्वाविमौ पुरुषौ लोके क्षरश्चाक्षर एव च ।
क्षरः सर्वाणि भूतानि कूटस्थोऽक्षर उच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
dvāvimau puruṣau loke kṣaraścākṣara eva ca
kṣaraḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni kūṭastho'kṣara ucyate
verses.essence: There are these two purushas in the world: the perishable and the imperishable. The perishable is all beings; the unchanging one is called the imperishable.
verses.translation: Two Purushas there are in this world, the perishable and the imperishable. All beings are the perishable and the Kutastha the unchanging is called the imperishable.

anvaya.meaning: these two purushas in the world
the perishable, and the imperishable
the perishable is all beings
the summit-seated (unchanging) is called imperishable

verses.explanation: The final taxonomy begins: kṣara, the perishable — every form, every body, the whole flowing world of beings; and akṣara, the kūṭastha — the anvil-still, unchanging principle, the witness-self that the flux cannot touch. Field and knower from chapter 13, restated as two 'persons.' The count seems complete: matter and spirit, two. The next verse adds what neither philosophy expected.
verses.connection: Restates ch13's pair as kshara and akshara — the setup for 15.17's third term.
verses.schools_note: The kshara-akshara pair sets up the chapter's climax, and its reading determines each school's map: Shankara — the perishable as all appearance, the kutastha as the unchanging witness-Self (with maya readings debated); Ramanuja — bound souls (kshara) and freed souls (akshara) as two real classes, the kutastha as the liberated. What none dispute: neither category is the summit; 15.17 was written to exceed them both.
verses.reflection: Sort one hour of your world into kshara and akshara — the flowing and the unmoved watching it flow.
verses.background: The summit of the chapter and one of the Gita's peaks: two Persons in the world, the perishable (all beings) and the imperishable (the unchanging) — but there is another, the Highest, called Paramatma, the imperishable Lord who enters and sustains the three worlds. Because I transcend the perishable and exceed even the imperishable, I am celebrated as Purushottama. Who knows Me thus, undeluded, worships Me with his whole being — and with this most secret teaching declared, one becomes wise and fulfilled.

verses.devanagari:
उत्तमः पुरुषस्त्वन्यः परमात्मेत्युधाहृतः ।
यो लोकत्रयमाविश्य बिभर्त्यव्यय ईश्वरः॥
verses.transliteration:
uttamaḥ puruṣastvanyaḥ paramātmetyudhāhṛtaḥ
yo lokatrayamāviśya bibhartyavyaya īśvaraḥ
verses.essence: But the highest Purusha is yet another, called the supreme Self — the imperishable Lord who, entering the three worlds, sustains them.
verses.translation: But distinct is the Supreme Purusha called the highest Self, the indestructible Lord Who, pervading the three worlds, sustains them.

anvaya.meaning: but the highest Purusha is another
declared as 'the supreme Self'
who, entering the triple world
sustains it — the imperishable Lord

verses.explanation: The third term: uttamaḥ puruṣas tv anyaḥ — *another*, beyond both the changing world and the changeless witness. Not the flux, not the stillness watching the flux, but the Lord who enters and carries both: āviśya bibharti, pervading and bearing. The two-story universe of Sankhya gains its missing resident — the owner of the whole house.
verses.connection: The doctrinal peak of the chapter: above matter and above spirit-as-witness stands the sustaining Lord.
verses.reflection: Then remember the third: not the flux, not the witness, but the Lord who enters and carries both.
verses.background: The summit of the chapter and one of the Gita's peaks: two Persons in the world, the perishable (all beings) and the imperishable (the unchanging) — but there is another, the Highest, called Paramatma, the imperishable Lord who enters and sustains the three worlds. Because I transcend the perishable and exceed even the imperishable, I am celebrated as Purushottama. Who knows Me thus, undeluded, worships Me with his whole being — and with this most secret teaching declared, one becomes wise and fulfilled.

verses.devanagari:
यस्मात्क्षरमतीतोऽहमक्षरादपि चोत्तमः ।
अतोऽस्मि लोके वेदे च प्रथितः पुरुषोत्तमः॥
verses.transliteration:
yasmātkṣaramatīto'hamakṣarādapi cottamaḥ
ato'smi loke vedeca prathitaḥ puruṣottamaḥ
verses.essence: Since I transcend the perishable and am higher even than the imperishable, I am celebrated in the world and in the Veda as Purushottama — the Supreme Person.
verses.translation: As I transcend the perishable and am even higher than the imperishable, I am declared to be the highest Purusha in the world and in the Vedas.

anvaya.meaning: since I transcend the perishable
and am higher even than the imperishable
therefore I am, in the world and in the Veda
celebrated as Purushottama

verses.explanation: The self-identification the chapter is named for: the speaker is the third purusha. Atītaḥ... uttamaḥ — past the perishing world, and *above even the imperishable* witness-absolute. The charioteer holding the reins is declaring himself beyond both everything that changes and everything that doesn't. Etymology becomes theology: puruṣa-uttama, the uttermost Person, and the title was already waiting in the Veda.
verses.connection: Krishna steps into 15.17's vacancy by name — the verse that crowns the Gita's theology of the personal God.
verses.schools_note: The hinge of Vedantic debate. Advaita reads akshara here as the unmanifest (maya/avyakta), preserving the nondual Self as ultimate. Ramanuja reads akshara as the liberated soul-principle itself — making the personal Lord categorically supreme over both matter and souls. The Gaudiya school treats this verse as the Gita's final word on God's personhood.
verses.reflection: Address the Purushottama once by full title today — higher than the perishable, above the imperishable.
verses.background: The summit of the chapter and one of the Gita's peaks: two Persons in the world, the perishable (all beings) and the imperishable (the unchanging) — but there is another, the Highest, called Paramatma, the imperishable Lord who enters and sustains the three worlds. Because I transcend the perishable and exceed even the imperishable, I am celebrated as Purushottama. Who knows Me thus, undeluded, worships Me with his whole being — and with this most secret teaching declared, one becomes wise and fulfilled.

verses.devanagari:
यो मामेवमसम्मूढो जानाति पुरुषोत्तमम् ।
स सर्वविद्भजति मां सर्वभावेन भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
yo māmevamasammūḍho jānāti puruṣottamam
sa sarvavidbhajati māṃ sarvabhāvena bhārata
verses.essence: One who, undeluded, knows me thus as the Supreme Person — that knower of all worships me with whole being, Bharata.
verses.translation: He who, undeluded, knows Me thus as the highest Purusha, he, knowing all, worships Me with his whole being (heart), O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: who me, thus, undeluded
knows as the Purushottama
that one, all-knowing, worships me
with (his) whole being, O Bharata

verses.explanation: The response right knowledge produces: sarva-vit, such a one knows *everything that matters* — because the root of all has been found — and the knowing immediately overflows as bhajati, worship, sarva-bhāvena, with every fiber, every mood, the whole of one's being. In this chapter's logic, complete theology and complete love are the same event seen from two sides.
verses.connection: The fruit of 15.18: when the Supreme Person is truly known, whole-being devotion is not commanded but inevitable.
verses.reflection: Let right knowing overflow correctly today: worship with the whole being, sarva-bhavena, in one act.
verses.background: The summit of the chapter and one of the Gita's peaks: two Persons in the world, the perishable (all beings) and the imperishable (the unchanging) — but there is another, the Highest, called Paramatma, the imperishable Lord who enters and sustains the three worlds. Because I transcend the perishable and exceed even the imperishable, I am celebrated as Purushottama. Who knows Me thus, undeluded, worships Me with his whole being — and with this most secret teaching declared, one becomes wise and fulfilled.

verses.devanagari:
इति गुह्यतमं शास्त्रमिदमुक्तं मयानघ ।
एतद्बुद्ध्वा बुद्धिमान्स्यात्कृतकृत्यश्च भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
iti guhyatamaṃ śāstramidamuktaṃ mayānagha
etadbuddhvā buddhimānsyātkṛtakṛtyaśca bhārata
verses.essence: Thus has this most secret teaching been declared by me, sinless one. Awakening to this, one becomes truly wise, Bharata — one whose work is done.
verses.translation: Thus, this most secret science has been taught by Me, O sinless one; on knowing this, a man becomes wise, and all his duties are accomplished, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: thus, the most secret teaching
this has been spoken by me, O sinless one
awakening to this, one would become wise
and one-whose-task-is-accomplished, O Bharata

verses.explanation: The colophon of the shortest dense chapter: guhyatamam śāstram, the *most* secret of teachings — the inverted tree, the fragment-soul, the indwelling fire, the three purushas, all in twenty verses. And the reward is a word every weary person understands: kṛta-kṛtyaḥ — the one whose to-do list is finished forever, who has done the thing all other doings were circling. The search itself retires.
verses.connection: Seals the chapter as a complete scripture-in-miniature — before ch16 turns to those who refuse it.
verses.reflection: Read this chapter's twenty verses sometime as one breath — the shortest complete scripture you own.
verses.background: The summit of the chapter and one of the Gita's peaks: two Persons in the world, the perishable (all beings) and the imperishable (the unchanging) — but there is another, the Highest, called Paramatma, the imperishable Lord who enters and sustains the three worlds. Because I transcend the perishable and exceed even the imperishable, I am celebrated as Purushottama. Who knows Me thus, undeluded, worships Me with his whole being — and with this most secret teaching declared, one becomes wise and fulfilled.
